questions.question: For the KMP failure function, what does lps[j] = 3 mean for pattern[0..j]?
options: The pattern has 3 matching characters starting at position j
The longest proper prefix of pattern[0..j] that is also a suffix has length 3
There are exactly 3 occurrences of the pattern up to position j
The pattern can be shifted by 3 positions on mismatch
questions.explanation: The failure function lps[j] stores the length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..j] that is also a suffix of pattern[0..j]. A value of 3 means we can safely resume matching from pattern index 3 after a mismatch at position j.

questions.question: In the Z-algorithm, what does Z[i] = 5 indicate?
options: There are 5 matches of the pattern in the string
The substring starting at position i has length 5
The longest substring starting at i that matches a prefix of S has length 5
Position i is the 5th occurrence of a repeated character
questions.explanation: Z[i] is the length of the longest substring starting at position i that matches a prefix of the string S. Z[i] = 5 means S[i..i+4] == S[0..4].

questions.question: Why can Boyer-Moore examine fewer than n characters when searching a text of length n?
options: It skips characters that appear in both the text and the pattern
On a mismatch, the bad-character heuristic can shift the pattern by its full length, skipping many text positions without examining them
It only checks every other character using a hash function
It pre-sorts the text to enable binary search
questions.explanation: Boyer-Moore scans the pattern right-to-left. When a mismatched text character does not appear in the pattern, the bad-character heuristic shifts the pattern by its full length m, meaning the algorithm skips m positions without examining intermediate text characters. This gives sublinear average-case performance.

questions.question: What is the guaranteed worst-case time complexity of KMP search on a text of length n and pattern of length m?
options: O(n * m)
O(n log n)
O(n + m)
O(m * log n)
questions.explanation: KMP guarantees O(n + m) in all cases. Building the failure function takes O(m), and the search phase takes O(n) because the text pointer never moves backward — each text character is examined at most twice (once when matched, once when a mismatch forces a failure-function lookup).

questions.question: Why does the naive string matching algorithm have O(nm) worst-case complexity?
options: Because it sorts the pattern first
Because on each mismatch it shifts the pattern by one position, potentially comparing every character of the text with every character of the pattern
Because it uses extra memory proportional to the pattern length
Because it must scan the text twice
questions.explanation: The naive approach tries the pattern at every position in the text. On a mismatch at the last character, it shifts by one and starts over. For inputs like text='aaaa...a' and pattern='aaaab', almost every comparison fails at the last character, giving O(nm).
